intent: The SAME logical denial (httpd_t -> default_t:file { read getattr }) captured on el8 (kernel 4.18, policy.31) and el10 (kernel 6.12, policy.35), to prove cross-kernel parser fidelity. The two records differ ONLY in incidental framing the kernel's generic audit dumper controls (timestamp, serial, pid, dev= notation 'dm-0' vs 'nvme0n1p4', ino value); the SELinux-relevant tokens (verdict, perm brace, scontext/tcontext/tclass/permissive) are byte-identical, exactly per f4 section 4. Both must parse to the identical narrow allow.
_kinds_note: TeAllowable on BOTH variants (audit2why 'Missing type enforcement (TE) allow rule.' identical el8 and el10). floor none. The authoritative bucket is version-INVARIANT, as required.

el8.audit2allow_R: allow httpd_t default_t:file { getattr read }; (RAW allow - el8's selinux-policy-devel 3.14 macro library has NO narrowing interface for default_t:file, so -R fell back to the raw allow)

el10.audit2allow_R: files_read_default_files(httpd_t) (MACRO - el10's selinux-policy-devel 42 macro library DOES carry a files_read_default_files interface, so -R substituted it)

oracle.CROSS_VERSION_DIVERGENCE: audit2allow -N is IDENTICAL on el8 and el10 (`allow httpd_t default_t:file { getattr read };`). audit2allow -R DIVERGES: el8 emits the raw allow (no matching interface in the 3.14 macro set), el10 substitutes files_read_default_files(httpd_t) (the 42 macro set has the interface). This is the version-dependent macro-library divergence f4 section 4 predicts, and the headline reason RuleSteward emits RAW allows: the narrow transform (-N target) is version-invariant; only the refpolicy interface library -R depends on moves between releases. The SELinux RECORD FORMAT itself (scontext/tcontext/tclass/permissive layout) is byte-identical across el8 4.18 and el10 6.12.
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: el8: equal to -R (no macro fired). el10: strictly narrower than -R (omits files_read_default_files which pads to read_file_perms across all default_t files). expected.te is the same exact `{ read getattr }` on both.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: Two points. (1) PARSER FIDELITY: a parser that positionally hard-codes field offsets or assumes a fixed dev= format (numeric major:minor vs device-name vs nvme path) would mis-parse one kernel's record. The robust approach (f4 section 1.1) is to parse only the four guaranteed SELinux fields positionally and treat everything between 'for ' and 'scontext=' as optional k=v - then el8 and el10 parse identically (PROVEN: both yield the same allow). (2) The -R divergence is exactly why triage must NOT mirror audit2allow -R: the same denial gets a raw allow on el8 but a broad macro on el10, so an -R-based tool's output silently changes breadth by OS. RuleSteward's raw-allow output is version-portable.
